coredata.dc:title: A detailed study of physicochemical properties and microstructure of EmimCl-EG deep eutectic solvents: Their influence on SO2 absorption behavior
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jiahong
Xu, Yingjie
Feng, Xiao
Zhu, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: To get a better understanding of the effect of physicochemical properties and microstructure on SO2 absorption behavior of DESs with different molar ratios of EmimCl and EG (from 2:1 to 1:2), densities (ρ), viscosities (η), speeds of sound (u), refractive indices (nD ), and thermal decomposition temperatures (T d) of EmimCl-EG DESs were measured and used to obtain the other derived properties, such as thermal expansion coefficient (α p) and activation energy for viscous flow (Eη ). Moreover, FT-IR spectra and in situ variable-temperature NMR spectroscopy were employed to study the microstructures of DESs. Based on physicochemical and spectroscopic properties, the influence of the concentrations of EmimCl on the interactions in DESs was explored to be associated with their SO2 absorption behavior. The results show that the interactions between Emim+ and Cl− of EmimCl is gradually weakening with increasing the concentration of EG in DESs by forming of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl−, resulting in a decrease of ρ, η, u, n D, and T d of DESs, and hindering the charge–transfer interaction of SO2 with Cl− and deceasing SO2 capture capacity. Moreover, the SO2 absorption capacity of DESs is proportional to their ρ and Eη , respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Deep eutectic solvent
Microstructure
Physicochemical property
Spectroscopy
Sulfur dioxide absorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison of densities, refractive indices, and thermal stability of three EmimCl-EG DESs
Comparison of viscosities and speeds of sound of three EmimCl-EG DESs
Comparison of microstructure of three EmimCl-EG DESs proved by FT-IR and NMR spectra
The effect of physicochemical properties and microstructure on SO2 absorption behavior of EmimCl-EG DESs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Plot of ρ against T (A) and lnρ against T (B) for three EmimCl-EG DESs.
Comparison of the thermal stability of EG, EmimCl, and three EmimCl-EG DESs.
Plot of η against T by VFT equation (A) and lnη against T by Arrhenius equation (B) for three EmimCl-EG DESs.
Variations of speeds of sound with temperature for three EmimCl-EG DESs.
Comparison of FT-IR spectrum for the three EmimCl-EG DESs.
Comparison of 1H NMR (A) and 13C NMR (B) spectrum for three EmimCl-EG DESs.
Temperature-dependent 1H NMR spectroscopy of EmimCl-EG DESs. (A): EmimCl-EG (2:1); (B): EmimCl-EG (1:1); (C): EmimCl-EG (1:2).
Plot of 1H NMR chemical shifts of hydroxyl of three EmimCl-EG DESs against T.
Eη or ρ (T =293.15K) vs the measured SO2 capture capacity of three EmimCl-EG DESs [3].
Densities (ρ), viscosities (η), and speeds of sound (u) of EmimCl-EG DESs at T =293.15–333.15K.
Density-temperature model parameters, thermal expansion coefficient (α P), and thermal decomposition temperatures (T d) of EmimCl-EG DESs.
Fitted values of viscosity η 0, B, T 0, η ∞, Eη , and R 2 according to the VFT and Arrhenius equations.

head.ce:title: A detailed study of physicochemical properties and microstructure of EmimCl-EG deep eutectic solvents: Their influence on SO2 absorption behavior

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shaoxing University, Shaoxing, Zhejiang 312000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To get a better understanding of the effect of physicochemical properties and microstructure on SO2 absorption behavior of DESs with different molar ratios of EmimCl and EG (from 2:1 to 1:2), densities (ρ), viscosities (η), speeds of sound (u), refractive indices (nD ), and thermal decomposition temperatures (T d) of EmimCl-EG DESs were measured and used to obtain the other derived properties, such as thermal expansion coefficient (α p) and activation energy for viscous flow (Eη ). Moreover, FT-IR spectra and in situ variable-temperature NMR spectroscopy were employed to study the microstructures of DESs. Based on physicochemical and spectroscopic properties, the influence of the concentrations of EmimCl on the interactions in DESs was explored to be associated with their SO2 absorption behavior. The results show that the interactions between Emim+ and Cl− of EmimCl is gradually weakening with increasing the concentration of EG in DESs by forming of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl−, resulting in a decrease of ρ, η, u, n D, and T d of DESs, and hindering the charge–transfer interaction of SO2 with Cl− and deceasing SO2 capture capacity. Moreover, the SO2 absorption capacity of DESs is proportional to their ρ and Eη , respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Deep eutectic solvents (DESs) acknowledged as ionic liquids (ILs) analogues were first introduced by Abbot et al. in 2003 [1] and could be easily prepared by mixing two or more compounds without using other solvents [2]. Recently, DESs have received much attention from the area of green chemistry, such as absorption of acidic gas [3–6], extraction and separation processes [7–9], organic synthesis [10,11], and material chemistry [12,13], due to their unique properties, such as negligible vapor pressure, low price, easy preparation, and so on. For example, Yang et al. reported the absorption of SO2 by DESs formed by 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (EmimCl) and ethylene glycol (EG) with the different EmimCl-EG molar ratios (from 2:1 to 1:2), exhibiting an extremely high absorption capacity and a great potential for use in industry [3]. It is well-known that detail designing and optimization of industrial processes require thermodynamic and transport properties, and DESs is no exception [14,15]. For example, Wagle et al. reported that viscosity, surface tension, and hydrogen bond interactions of DESs played significant roles in modulating species reactivity and mass transport properties governing the genesis of nanostructure when using DESs as designer solvents to create well-defined nanomaterials [15]. Therefore, proper knowledge about physicochemical properties and the strength of hydrogen bond interaction of DESs will be useful for applications in industrial and chemical processes.

In recent years, many researches have focused attention on physicochemical properties of DESs formed by a quaternary ammonium salt as a hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) with metal salts or a hydrogen bond donor (HBD) such as urea, glycerol, carbohydrate-derived polyols or renewably sourced carboxylic acids, and the results demonstrated that viscosity and conductivity of those DESs were similar to ILs and the hydrogen bond interaction between HBD and HBA had a great influence on the structure and properties of DESs [15–20]. For instance, in our previous work, the influence of electronic effect of substitution group of HBD on hydrogen bond interaction of DESs formed by choline chloride (ChCl) and phenolic compounds (phenol, p-cresol, and p-chlorophenol) have been investigated by FT-IR spectra and physicochemical properties, exhibiting that the strength of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl− between ChCl and phenolic compounds is as follows: p-chlorophenol>phenol> p-cresol, moreover, and the stronger the hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl− leads to the higher ρ and n D of DESs [20]. Compared with the traditional DESs, the research about physicochemical properties and microstructure of the emerging DESs is still few. For example, Yang et al. found that the SO2 absorption behavior of EmimCl-EG DESs was associated with the concentration of EmimCl in DESs, indicating that the interactions between EmimCl and EG may affect the SO2 absorption property of EmimCl-EG DESs [3]. However, a detailed research concerning the influence of the concentration of EmimCl in DESs on the interactions and physicochemical properties of EmimCl-EG DESs still needs to be further investigated. It is well-known that the composition and concentration of materials played key roles in controlling the morphologies, properties, and applications [21–28]. Therefore, when EmimCl-EG DESs are employed for SO2 absorption in industry or other applications, it is also necessary to understand their physicochemical properties and microstructure, due to their significant influence on SO2 absorption behavior [3].

1-Ethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (98.0+%) was obtained from Alfa Aesar. Ethylene glycol anhydrous (99.8%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. All the chemicals were used directly as received. Three EmimCl-EG DESs with different EmimCl-EG molar ratios (from 2:1 to 1:2) were prepared simply by stirring a EmimCl and EG mixture at 343.2K until a homogeneous liquid was formed, similar to the preparation process reported in literature [3]. The water contents of DESs, detected by a Karl Fischer Coulometric titration (Mettler Toledo C20S), were found to be less than 800ppm for three EmimCl-EG DESs.

Densities, speeds of sound, and viscosities of three EmimCl-EG DESs were simultaneously measured with an Anton Paar DSA 5000M density and sound velocity meter combined with an Anton Paar AMVn falling ball automated microviscometer. The densimeter was calibrated with doubly distilled deionised water and dried air after self-check, and the temperature of the apparatus was controlled to within ±0.01K by a built-in Peltier device that corresponds to an uncertainty in the density of ±0.0002% [20]. Calibration of microviscometer was carried out using doubly distilled deionised water or viscosity standard oils (No. H117; Anton Paar Co). A calibrated capillary with diameters of 1.59mm was used in the present experiment. The measurement cell temperature of microviscometer was controlled by a built-in precise Peltier thermostat with a precision of T =±0.05K. The accuracy of density, speed of sound, and viscosity measurement was greater than ±5.0×10−5 gcm−3, ±0.01ms−1, and ±0.02mPas, respectively. An Abbe refractometer produced by Shanghai Precision Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd. (Model WAY-2S) with a measuring accuracy of ±1.0×10−4 was employed to measure the refractive index, which was calibrated by measuring the refractive index of the high-purity water. The temperature of the measurement of n D was controlled by a circulating-water bath with the accuracy of ±0.05K. Thermal decomposition temperature (T d) was measured with a TGA 2950 under N2 of 60ml/min at 10°C/min.

FT-IR spectra of three EmimCl-EG DESs were recorded on the range of 4000–600cm−1 at room temperature (∼298K) using a Nicolet Model 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer, equipped with a DTGS detector. An attenuated total reflection (ATR) accessory was employed in the experiment, which was made of Ge crystal with incident angles of 60° and 7 reflections. Each ATR-IR spectrum was recorded with a resolution of 2cm−1, a zero filling factor of 2, and 16 parallel scans [33]. 1H NMR and 13C NMR of three EmimCl-EG DESs were measured using a Bruker Model AV-400 spectrometer. To avoid the influence of deuterated reagents on the 1H NMR and 13C NMR chemical shifts of sample, the internal reference method was adopted according to our previous work [29,34,35]. Specific measurement process was as follows: the deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO-d 6) was sealed in a 2-mm capillary tube, which was placed at the center of a 5-mm sample tube filled with the sample and chemical shift reference of tetramethylsilane (TMS). Moreover, in situ variable-temperature 1H NMR spectroscopy of three EmimCl-EG DESs was recorded on at 25, 30, 40, 50, and 60°C to investigate hydrogen bond interaction between EmimCl and EG. The temperature was controlled by a built-in precise thermostat through the software, going from low to high temperature with heating rate of 2°Cmin−1 and holding time of 10min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of densities, refractive indices, and thermal stability of three EmimCl-EG DESs
ce:section.ce:para:
Density is an important physical property due to its effect on the design and operation of processes [36]. Thus, it is essential to identify the behavior of density with respect to temperature. In this study, the densities (ρ) of three EmimCl-EG DESs were measured in temperature range of 293.15–333.15K, which are presented in Table 1 and Fig. 1 A. As expected, one can see that the value of ρ is decreasing with increasing temperature. In general, the decrease of density as temperature increases may be due to the increased activity and molecular mobility, which increase the molar volume of the solution, thereby reducing the density [37]. The experimental data of the density ρ as a function of temperature T were fitted by Eq. (1):
(1)
ρ = a T + b
where a and b are constants that represent the molar ratio of DES to HBD [20,37], which are provided in Table 2 . The regression coefficient R 2 is found to be better than 0.9995, indicating that the density-temperature relationship is linear, which is illustrated in Fig. 1A.

As can be seen in Fig. 1A, the order of ρ for three EmimCl-EG DESs at the same temperature is as follows: EmimCl-EG (2:1)>EmimCl-EG (1:1)>EmimCl-EG (1:2), which seems to suggest that the concentration of EmimCl in DESs may have a significant effect on the density and the microstructure of DESs. For pure EmimCl, there are strong electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond interactions between Emim+ and Cl−. With the addition of EG in EmimCl, the interactions between cation and anion of EmimCl is weakened gradually by forming of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl−, which leads to the structure of EmimCl become loose and increase the molar volume of EmimCl-EG DESs. Therefore, the decease of density of three EmimCl-EG DESs with molar ratios of EmimCl to EG from 2:1 to 1: 2 may imply that the interactions of Emim+ and Cl− become weaker with increasing the concentration of EG in DESs.

The thermal expansion coefficient α P, also known as volume expansivity, was usually used to evaluate the free volumes or interstices of ILs, which was closely related to CO2 or SO2 solubility in ILs [38–40]. Because of similarity to ILs, it is important to study of the thermal expansion coefficient of DESs, which can be calculated by following equation:
(2)
α p
=
1 V
(
∂ V
∂ T
)
p
= −
(
∂ ln ρ
∂ T
)
p
where V is the molar volume. To obtain α P, the relationship between the lnρ and T of three EmimCl-EG DESs were fitted by the least-square method and shown in Fig. 1B. According to the slope of the linear equation, then the α P for three DESs could be obtained and listed in Table 2, and the R 2 was found to be better than 0.9995. As listed in Table 2, one can see that α P value of three EmimCl-EG DESs falls between 5.20×10−4 and 5.70×10−4, which is smaller than that of DESs formed by ChCl and phenolic compounds [20], and similar to that of 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium ethyl sulfate [41]. As shown in Table 2, one can see that the sequence of α P is as follows: EmimCl-EG (2:1)<EmimCl-EG (1:1)<EmimCl-EG (1:2), indicating that free volumes or interstices of EmimCl-EG (2:1) is a little less than that of EmimCl-EG (1:1) or EmimCl-EG (1:2), due to the stronger interactions in EmimCl-EG (2:1) DESs.

The free volumes or interstices of mixture can be characterized by the refractive index n D, defined as the ratio of the speed of light in vacuo c 0 to the speed of light in the material c, is one of the most fundamental properties of pure liquids and their solutions [42]. It is known that this optical property can be used as a measure of the electronic polarizability of a molecule and can provide useful information when studying the interaction or free space between molecules [43–45]. As a consequence, n D is highly relevant for ILs due to the electrostatic interactions between cation and anion [45]. For three studied EmimCl-EG DESs with molar ratios of EmimCl to EG from 2:1 to 1: 2, n D at T =303.15K is 1.4905, 1.4842, and 1.4710, respectively, indicating the electrostatic interactions of EmimCl decreases with increasing the concentration of EG in DESs, due to form hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl−. As a result, light will travel at a stronger velocity in EmimCl-EG DESs with high EG concentration, and its refractive index will become smaller than EmimCl-EG DESs with low EG concentration. Therefore, it can be deduced that free volumes or interstices of EmimCl-EG DESs increases with increasing the concentration of EG in DESs, resulting from the weakening of the interactions between Emim+ and Cl−, which is in good agreement with that obtained from ρ or α P.

The thermal stability of DESs is very important because high stability helps DESs achieve long-term utilization for SO2 absorption and desorption. By comparing the thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) curve illustrated in Fig. 2 , one can see that the order of thermal stability of EG, EmimCl, and EmimCl-EG DESs is as follows: EmimCl>EmimCl-EG (2:1)>EmimCl-EG (1:1)>EmimCl-EG (1:2)>EG, decreasing with the concentration of EG in DESs, due to the weakening of the interactions between Emim+ and Cl− by forming of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl−. As can be seen from Table 2, the decomposition temperatures of three EmimCl-EG DESs range from 375K to 480K.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of viscosities and speeds of sound of three EmimCl-EG DESs
ce:section.ce:para: Like most of the ILs, viscosity of DESs is an important issue that needs to be addressed, especially for equipment design and fluid flow calculations. Herein, the viscosities of three EmimCl-EG DESs were measured at the temperature range of 293.15–333.15K. As listed in Table 1, one can see that η values of three EmimCl-EG DESs at T =293.15K falls between 16 and 21mPas, which are much lower than that of DESs formed by ChCl and phenolic compounds [20], and smaller than that of 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate [46]. The relative low viscosity of three studied DESs benefits to their industrial application. As shown in Table 1, one can see that the sequence of η is as follows: EmimCl-EG (2:1)>EmimCl-EG (1:1)>EmimCl-EG (1:2), due to the dissociation of the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond networks of EmimCl with the addition of EG.

The temperature dependence of viscosity for three DESs is shown in Fig. 3 A, which presents an exponential decrease as temperature increases, indicating that η value is more affected by temperature than the other studied properties such as ρ and u, due to the hydrogen bond interactions between Emim+ and Cl− or between EmimCl and EG weakening as temperature rises. The temperature dependence of viscosity for DESs was fitted by using Vogel–Fulcher–Tamman (VFT) equation, which could be expressed as follows [47,48]:
(3)
η =
η 0
exp (
B /
( T −
T 0
)
)
where η 0, B, and T 0 are empirical constants. The best fit values of η 0, B, and T 0 and the correlation coefficient, R 2, are listed in Table 3 .

Usually, the Arrhenius equation is also used to fit the dynamic viscosity [36] and the equation is:
(4)
η =
η ∞
exp (
E η
/ R T )
where Eη is the activation energy for viscous flow, η ∞ is an empirical constant, and R is ideal gas constant. By fitting lnη against T −1 (Fig. 3B), then Eη and η ∞ of three EmimCl-EG DESs can be obtained, which are also listed in Table 3. From Table 3, the values of the correlation coefficient, R 2, of three DESs based on the VFT equation are similar to those based on Arrhenius equation, indicating that both two equations can be suitable for application to the studied DESs. Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 3, the fitted values are in better agreement with experimental ones. One can see that Eη value of three studied DESs is typically about 24kJmol−1, which much less than the value of DESs formed by zinc chloride and donor molecules such as urea and acetamide [49] and DESs formed by ChCl and phenolic compounds [20]. The small value of Eη means that the studied DESs have perfect fluidity, which is good for improvement absorption rate of SO2. The results of absorption of SO2 by EmimCl-EG DESs demonstrated that they have great absorption rate of SO2 at T =293.15K and 1.0atm [3], reaching the absorption equilibrium with 10min, which may be attributed to their small values of Eη .

The temperature dependence of speed of sound for three EmimCl-EG DESs is shown in Fig. 4 and Table 1. Comparing the speed of sound of three EmimCl-EG DESs, it can be found that the sequence of u at the same temperature is as follows: EmimCl-EG (2:1)>EmimCl-EG (1:1)>EmimCl-EG (1:2), which is consistent with the tendency of ρ, η, n D, and T d, suggesting that DESs is becoming more compact with increasing the concentration of EmimCl, it could be mainly attributed due to the strong electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond interactions between Emim+ and Cl−. As temperature rise, the interactions between Emim+ and Cl− or between EmimCl and EG are weakening, resulting in a decrease of speed of sound for three EmimCl-EG DESs. Moreover, a good linear relation between speed of sound and temperature can be found in Fig. 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of microstructure of three EmimCl-EG DESs proved by FT-IR and NMR spectra
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectrum is usually utilized to study the interaction of different groups in order to analyze and identify the structure of a material [29–33,50], especially for characterizing the hydrogen bond interactions because of the sensitivity to the change of microstructure [51,52]. Unlike traditional DESs, the studied EmimCl-EG DESs not only existed the hydrogen bond interactions between EmimCl and EG, but also coexisted the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond interactions between Emim+ and Cl− of EmimCl simultaneously. According to the results obtained from physicochemical properties of EmimCl-EG DESs, we can know that the interactions in EmimCl-EG DESs, such as the interaction between Emim+ and Cl− and the interaction of OH⋯Cl−, play an important role in the microstructure of system, which will influence their SO2 absorption behavior [3]. Therefore, it is essential to study the microstructure of three EmimCl-EG DESs.

In this work, FT-IR analyses were performed to observe the functional groups and the shifts of the hydroxyl group of EG and aromatic protons of EmimCl were focused on to evaluate the strength of hydrogen bond interactions in EmimCl-EG DESs, due to their ability to form hydrogen bond interactions. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the shifts of IR absorption peaks of three EmimCl-EG DESs mainly occurs about 2500–3600cm−1, whereas the IR absorption peaks at 750–2500cm−1 remain nearly unchanged with increasing the concentration. Peaks at 3330–3360cm−1 of three EmimCl-EG DESs can be assigned to the stretching vibration of OH functional group of EG after the formation of the DESs with EmimCl. As can be seen in Fig. 5A, the IR absorption peaks of the stretching vibration of OH functional group of EmimCl-EG DESs shift to lower wavenumber and show an obvious red-shifting with increasing the concentration of EG in DESs, which suggests that OH functional group of EG takes part in the formation of the hydrogen bond interaction with anion of EmimCl of OH⋯Cl−, resulting in the disassociation of the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond interactions between Emim+ and Cl−. Peaks about 3080–3160cm−1 of three DESs can be ascribed to CH stretching vibration of aromatic ring of EmimCl after the formation of the DESs, among which peaks about 3145–3155cm−1 is belonged to the stretching vibration of C4,5–H of Emim+, and peaks about 3085–3105cm−1 is belonged to the stretching vibration of C2–H of Emim+ [53]. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, one can see that the IR absorption peaks of the stretching vibration of CH of Emim+ shifts to larger wavenumber and shows a slight blue-shifting with increasing the concentration of EG in DESs, indicating that the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond interactions between Emim+ and Cl− become increasingly weaker with the addition of EG in EmimCl, which is consistent with the results obtained from IR absorption peaks of OH of EG.

1H NMR and 13C NMR spectroscopy of three EmimCl-EG DESs at 298.15K is shown in Fig. 6 , which is in good agreement with the literature [3]. As shown in Fig. 6, 13C NMR chemical shifts of three EmimCl-EG DESs are almost the same, indicating that the concentration of EG in DESs has little effect on 13C NMR chemical shift, which is attributed to the fact that carbon atoms seldom participate in intermolecular interactions directly. Unlike 13C NMR chemical shifts, 1H NMR chemical shifts of studied DESs exhibit change with the concentration of EG in DESs, especially for 1H NMR chemical shifts of aromatic proton (located at 7–10ppm), hydroxyl proton (located at 4–5ppm), and methyl proton of cation (located at 0.5–1 and 2–3ppm), suggesting that the change of 1H NMR chemical shifts can be employed to investigate the microstructure of studied EmimCl-EG DESs with the concentration. As can be seen in Fig. 6A, 1H NMR chemical shifts of aromatic proton and hydroxyl proton of three EmimCl-EG DESs decrease with the increasing of EG in DESs, whereas those of methyl proton of cation increase with the increasing of EG in DESs, which seems to suggest that the interactions between Emim+ and Cl− gradually weaken by forming of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl−, and the physical interactions between methyl groups of Emim+ and EG strengthen consequently.

To confirm the above assumption, in situ variable-temperature 1H NMR spectroscopy is employed to investigate the microstructure of EmimCl-EG DESs, because the sensitivity of hydrogen bond interaction, electrostatic interaction, and physical interaction to temperature is inconsistent [29,54]. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , 1H NMR chemical shifts of hydroxyl proton of EG in three EmimCL-EG DESs have the most significant changes compared to other peaks, moving to high-field positions gradually with the increasing of temperature. The behavior indicates that EG can interact with EmimCl by formation of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl− in EmimCl-EG DESs. Furthermore, the strength of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl− weakens with the increasing of temperature and concentration of EmimCl in DESs. The relationship between 1H NMR chemical shifts of hydroxyl proton of EG and temperature is plotted in Fig. 8 , showing a good linear relationship and decreasing with the increase of temperature. Whereas 1H NMR chemical shifts of aromatic proton exhibit very slight variations with the temperature increasing compared to those of hydroxyl proton of EG (Fig. 7), indicating that those aromatic proton of Emim+ is dominated by electrostatic interaction, which is low sensitive to temperature [55,56]. 1H NMR chemical shifts of methyl proton of Emim+ remain unchanged with temperature may be attributed to physical interaction between methyl proton and EG. Therefore, a combination of IR and NMR spectroscopy, it can confirm that the strength of electrostatic interactions between Emim+ and Cl− of EmimCl gradually weaken with the addition of EG by forming of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl− and physical interaction between EG and methyl proton of Emim+ after the formation of the DESs, which is in agreement with the results obtained from the physicochemical properties, resulting in a decrease of ρ, η, u, n D, and T d of EmimCl-EG DESs with ratios of EmimCl and EG from 2:1 to 1:2.

By comparing the FT-IR spectra of three EmimCl-EG DESs, the change in the microstructure of DESs with increasing the concentration of EG in DESs can be speculated. The strength of electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond interactions between Emim+ and Cl− of EmimCl weaken gradually with the addition of EG by formation OH⋯Cl− hydrogen bond interaction after the formation of the DESs, which leads to ρ, η, u, n D, and T d of DESs decreases with ratios of EmimCl and EG from 2:1 to 1:2. Moreover, after the addition of EG in EmimCl, Cl− not only can interact with Emim+, but also can form of hydrogen bond interaction with OH of EG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of physicochemical properties and microstructure on SO2 absorption behavior of EmimCl-EG DESs
ce:section.ce:para: The SO2 absorption mechanism of EmimCl-EG DESs indicates that the SO2 absorption capacity is mainly determined by the charge–transfer interaction of the electropositive sulfur atom of SO2 with Cl− in DESs [3,57,58]. Therefore, the charge of Cl− of EmimCl-EG DESs, which is closely related to the microstructure of the system, may have an important role in SO2 absorption behavior. According to IR and NMR spectroscopy of EmimCl-EG DESs, after the addition of EG in EmimCl, Cl− not only interacts with Emim+, but also forms of hydrogen bond interaction with OH of EG, which will hinder the charge–transfer interaction of the electropositive sulfur atom of SO2 with Cl− and lower SO2 capture capacity [3,57,58]. The SO2 absorption experiment of EmimCl-EG DESs reported by Dai and co-workers has confirmed that the SO2 absorption capacity of EmimCl-EG DESs decreases with increasing the concentration of EG in DESs, and EmimCl is very important to improve the SO2 absorption capacity DESs [3], due to the stronger hydrogen bond interaction between EG and EmimCl reducing the interaction between EmimCl and SO2.

According to literature [3], the three studied EmimCl-EG DESs exhibit high SO2 absorption capacity, the SO2 solubilities in EmimCl-EG (1:2), EmimCl-EG (1:1), and EmimCl-EG (2:1) are 0.82g (45wt%), 1.03g (51wt%), and 1.15g (53wt%) of SO2 per gram of solvent, respectively, which increases with increasing the concentration of EmimCl in DESs. Based on the analysis of physicochemical properties of EmimCl-EG DESs, we have known that physicochemical properties of EmimCl-EG DESs also decrease with the increasing of EG in DESs, due to the formation of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl−, which will hinder the charge-transfer interaction of SO2 with Cl− and decrease SO2 capture capacity of EmimCl-EG DESs. Therefore, we suspect that there exists some relationship between SO2 absorption capacity and their physicochemical properties of the studied EmimCl-EG DESs. Consequently, the relationship between the saturated SO2 absorption capacity at T =293.15K [3] and ρ (T =293.15K) or Eη of three EmimCl-EG DESs is plotted in Fig. 9 . As illustrated in Fig. 9, we are surprised to find that the saturated SO2 absorption capacity of three EmimCl-EG DESs has a good linear relation with ρ or Eη , which is proportional to ρ or Eη . However, further investigation is needed to see if the results derived in this article apply to other DESs such as EmimCl-TEG during SO2 capture [57].

In this work, physicochemical properties of three EmimCl-EG DESs with different molar ratios of EmimCl and EG (from 2:1 to 1:2), such as densities, viscosities, refractive indices, speeds of sound, and thermal decomposition temperatures, combined with IR and NMR spectroscopic properties were investigated to be associated with their SO2 absorption behavior. IR and in situ variable-temperature NMR spectroscopy indicate that EmimCl can interact with EG by forming of hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl−. Furthermore, the interactions between Emim+ and Cl− of EmimCl become increasingly weaken with the addition of EG in EmimCl after formation of the EmimCl-EG DESs, whereas the hydrogen bond interaction of OH⋯Cl− is strengthened gradually, which leads to ρ, η, u, n D, and T d of DESs decreasing with ratios of EmimCl and EG from 2:1 to 1:2, and hinder the charge–transfer interaction of SO2 with Cl− and decrease SO2 capture capacity of DESs. Moreover, the SO2 absorption capacity of three EmimCl-EG DESs is proportional to their ρ and Eη , respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (no. Y18B060014), National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21406140), Undergraduate Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Shaoxing City (no. 2016R06) and Zhejiang Province (no. 2016R428031), the Key Teacher Training Research of Shaoxing University (no. 2016227).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular Theory of Gases and Liquids
